criteria: The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the PDBbind 2016 dataset composition: general set (9221 pairs), refined set (3685 pairs), core 2016 set (290 pairs), test105 (105 pairs), and test71 (71 pairs).

The agent has read Section 2.3 completely to understand the four input representations: label-encoded SMILES strings, label-encoded protein sequences, AG-FPs for compounds, and pocket descriptors from DoGSiteScorer.

The agent has read Section 2.4 completely to understand the three-part network architecture: embedding module (CNN + linear), dual-interaction module (highway + multihead attention), and regression prediction module.

The agent has read Section 2.4.2.1 to understand the highway block, including the transform gate T (sigmoid), carry gate C = 1 - T, nonlinear activation H (ReLU), and the bit-wise interaction of concatenated features.

The agent has read Section 2.4.2.2 to understand the multihead attention block, including Q/K/V projections, scaled dot-product attention (softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V), and the vector-wise interaction of stacked features.

The agent has read Table 2 to understand the main comparison results of DataDTA vs. Pafnucy, TopologyNet, DeepDTAF (native and predicted), DeepDTA, FusionDTA, and MFR-DTA on core 2016, test105, and test71 datasets.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the ablation study comparing DataDTA against four variants: without dual-interaction module, without AG-FPs, without pockets, and with top-1 pocket only.

The agent has read Section 3.3 and Figure 4 to understand the parameter sensitivity analysis including SMILES sequence length (90–210), protein sequence length (500–2000), learning rate, batch size, and CNN filter size.

The plan includes data preparation using PDBbind version 2016 with the training set of 11906 samples (9221 general + 2685 from refined), 1000 validation samples from the refined set, and 290 test samples from the core 2016 set.
The plan includes fixed-length encoding for SMILES strings (120 characters) and protein sequences (1000 amino acids), with truncation for longer inputs and zero-padding for shorter inputs.
The plan includes computation of 900-dimensional AG-FPs for each drug molecule using element-specific weighted colored Laplacian matrices for 10 chemical elements (H, C, N, O, F, P, S, Cl, Br, I).

The plan includes extraction of pocket descriptors using DoGSiteScorer via ProteinsPlus, retaining the top-3 predicted pockets (with zero-filling for fewer than 3) and extracting 42 descriptors per pocket to form a 126-dimensional vector.

The plan includes the CNN embedding module with dilated convolution residual blocks for protein sequences and drug SMILES, projecting outputs to 256 dimensions, and linear layers for AG-FPs and pocket descriptors also projecting to 256 dimensions.

The plan includes the dual-interaction aggregation module with two branches: a highway block operating on concatenated features (bit-wise) and a multihead attention block operating on stacked features (vector-wise).
The plan includes the regression prediction module with three fully connected linear layers: Linear(256*4, 1024, 521, 1), taking the accumulated outputs of the highway and attention branches.
The plan includes evaluation using all five metrics: CI, MSE, RMSE, Pearson R, and SD, with MSE used as the training loss function.

The plan includes comparison against competing methods: DeepDTA, Pafnucy, TopologyNet, DeepDTAF (both native and predicted pocket variants), FusionDTA, and MFR-DTA, all evaluated on core 2016, test105, and test71.

The plan includes the ablation study with four model variants: (i) without dual-interaction module, (ii) without AG-FPs, (iii) without pockets, and (iv) with only the top-1 pocket, all tested on the core 2016 dataset.
The plan specifies training each model configuration 5 times to reduce bias from randomness and saving the best model based on validation set performance.
The plan specifies the key hyperparameters: learning rate = 0.0001, CNN filter size = 256, SMILES fixed length = 120, protein sequence fixed length = 1000.
The code for loading and preprocessing the PDBbind 2016 dataset (general, refined, core 2016, test105, test71 sets) with deduplication and overlap removal has been implemented.

The code for constructing the training set (11906 samples = 9221 general + 2685 from refined), validation set (1000 from refined), and core 2016 test set (290 samples) has been implemented without overlap.
The code for label encoding of drug SMILES strings to integer sequences of fixed length 120, with longer sequences truncated and shorter sequences zero-padded, has been implemented.
The code for label encoding of protein sequences to integer sequences of fixed length 1000 amino acids, with longer sequences truncated and shorter sequences zero-padded, has been implemented.

The code for computing AG-FPs has been implemented: element-specific weighted colored Laplacian matrices are constructed for 10 chemical elements (H, C, N, O, F, P, S, Cl, Br, I), and five statistics (sum, min, max, mean, std) of nontrivial eigenvalues are extracted per element, yielding a 900-dimensional vector per drug molecule.

The code for extracting pocket descriptors via DoGSiteScorer has been implemented: top-3 predicted pockets are retained (zero-padded for fewer than 3 pockets), each described by 42 features (7 size/shape, 5 functional group, 6 element, 4 amino acid composition, 20 individual amino acid compositions), yielding a 126-dimensional input vector.

The code for the CNN embedding module with dilated convolution residual blocks has been implemented as Equations (1) and (2), with dilation rates l = 2^i (i = 0, 1, ..., n-2) enabling exponential receptive field expansion for protein sequences and drug SMILES.
The residual block in the CNN module replaces traditional convolution with dilated convolution and includes a shortcut connection (skip connection), projecting the output to m = 256 dimensions.
The code for projecting pocket descriptors to 256 dimensions has been implemented as Equation (3): H^P = Linear(126, 256).
The code for projecting AG-FPs to 256 dimensions has been implemented as Equation (4): H^C = Linear(900, 256).
The code for concatenating the four 256-dimensional feature vectors (H^D, H^S, H^P, H^C) into a 1024-dimensional vector x1 as the highway block input has been implemented as Equation (5).
The code for stacking the four 256-dimensional feature vectors (H^D, H^S, H^P, H^C) into a 4×256 matrix x2 as the multihead attention input has been implemented as Equation (6).

The code for the highway block has been implemented as Equation (9): y1 = H(x, W_H) * T(x, W_T) + x * C(x, W_C), where T is a sigmoid transform gate, C = 1 - T is the carry gate, and H is ReLU, enabling bit-wise adaptive feature transformation.

The code for the multihead attention block has been implemented as Equations (10)-(12): MultiHeadAttention(Q, K, V) = Concat(head_i, ..., head_b) * W^0, where head_i = Attention(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V) and Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V.
The code for computing the output O2 of the multihead attention block with ReLU activation has been implemented as Equation (13): y2 = H(MultiHeadAttention(Q, K, V)).
The code for accumulating (element-wise summing) the highway output O1 and the multihead attention output O2 before passing to the regression module has been implemented.

The code for the regression prediction module has been implemented as Equation (14): three fully connected linear layers with dimensions Linear(256*4=1024, 1024, 521, 1), producing the final scalar binding affinity prediction.
Layer normalization and dropout mechanisms have been interspersed throughout the network to improve training stability and generalization.

The code for computing the CI (concordance index) metric has been implemented as Equation (15): CI = (1/Z) * sum_{y_i > y_j} h(p_i - p_j), with step function h(x) = 1, 0.5, or 0 for x > 0, x = 0, or x < 0 respectively.
The code for the MSE loss function has been implemented as Equation (16): MSE = (1/n) * sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_i - y_i)^2, and is used as the training loss.
The code for computing the Pearson correlation coefficient R metric has been implemented as Equation (17), measuring the linear correlation between predicted and actual binding affinity values.
The code for computing the SD (standard deviation in regression) metric has been implemented as Equation (18): SD = sqrt((1/N) * sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - (m*p_i + n))^2).
The code for training DataDTA 5 independent times and selecting the best checkpoint based on validation set performance for final testing has been implemented.

The ablation variant without the dual-interaction module (highway block and multihead attention block) has been implemented as a model that directly feeds the four encoded features to the regression module.
The ablation variant without AG-FPs has been implemented using only protein sequences, SMILES strings, and pocket descriptors as inputs.
The ablation variant without pocket descriptors has been implemented using only protein sequences, SMILES strings, and AG-FPs as inputs.
The ablation variant with only the top-1 predicted pocket (instead of top-3) has been implemented, producing a 42-dimensional pocket feature vector.

The DeepDTAF baseline has been retrained with predicted pockets: top-1 predicted pocket structure files converted to pocket sequences of fixed length 55 characters (covering ~90% of pockets), using the same network parameters as the original Wang et al. (2021) publication.
The FusionDTA baseline has been retrained using the PDBbind 2016 data from this work, with parameters consistent with Yuan et al. (2022).
The MFR-DTA baseline has been retrained using the PDBbind 2016 data from this work, with parameters consistent with Hua et al. (2023).
The code for the parameter analysis experiments on SMILES string fixed length has been implemented, testing lengths of 90, 120, 150, 180, and 210 characters.
The code for the parameter analysis experiments on protein sequence fixed length has been implemented, testing lengths of 500, 1000, 1500, and 2000 amino acids.
The code for the parameter analysis experiments on CNN filter size has been implemented, comparing filter sizes of 128, 256, and 512.

The DataDTA model has been trained on the combined training set (11906 samples) and successfully evaluated on the core 2016 test dataset (290 samples), test105 dataset (105 samples), and test71 dataset (71 samples) without errors.
The four ablation study variants (without dual-interaction, without AG-FPs, without pockets, with top-1 pocket) have been trained and evaluated on the core 2016 test dataset without errors.
All competing baseline methods (Pafnucy, TopologyNet, DeepDTAF native and predicted, DeepDTA, FusionDTA, MFR-DTA) have been evaluated on all three test datasets without errors.
The SMILES string length parameter analysis experiments (90, 120, 150, 180, 210 characters) have been executed successfully on the validation dataset.
The protein sequence length parameter analysis experiments (500, 1000, 1500, 2000 amino acids) have been executed successfully.
The learning rate, batch size, and CNN filter size parameter analysis experiments have been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 for DataDTA on the core 2016 test dataset: CI = 0.806, MSE = 1.012, RMSE = 1.274, R = 0.814, SD = 1.265.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 for DataDTA on the test105 dataset: CI = 0.746, MSE = 1.127, RMSE = 1.405, R = 0.676, SD = 1.316.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2 for DataDTA on the test71 dataset: CI = 0.688, MSE = 0.949, RMSE = 1.220, R = 0.538, SD = 1.146.
The execution outcomes confirm that DataDTA achieves the best R value (0.814) and best CI (0.806) among all compared methods on the core 2016 test dataset.

The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: the variant without the dual-interaction module achieves CI = 0.790, MSE = 1.098, R = 0.781, SD = 1.360, RMSE = 1.368 on the core 2016 test dataset.
The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: the variant without AG-FPs achieves CI = 0.804, MSE = 1.038, R = 0.804, SD = 1.294, RMSE = 1.300 on the core 2016 test dataset.
The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: the variant without pockets achieves CI = 0.801, MSE = 1.042, R = 0.799, SD = 1.308, RMSE = 1.314 on the core 2016 test dataset.
The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 3: the variant with top-1 pocket only achieves CI = 0.802, MSE = 1.038, R = 0.802, SD = 1.299, RMSE = 1.305 on the core 2016 test dataset.

The execution outcomes confirm that removing the dual-interaction module causes the greatest performance degradation among all ablation variants, validating the importance of the highway+attention fusion strategy.
The execution outcomes match Figure 4: the SMILES length of 120 achieves the best validation CI (~0.8023), with performance increasing from 90 to 120 characters and remaining stable thereafter.
The execution outcomes confirm that a learning rate of 0.0001 achieves the best performance and that CNN filter size 256 yields slightly better CI and lower RMSE/MSE/SD than filter sizes 128 or 512.
